key_behaviors: Provide Maven project fixtures that run specific plugin goals (generate, validate, freeze) with configured checksumMode and plugin flags to exercise end-to-end flows.
Supply expected outputs (lockfile.json, pom.lockfile.expected.xml) that the integration tests compare with generated artifacts to assert correctness of freezing and lockfile generation.
Model parent/child POM resolution scenarios: relative parent paths, external parent (remote) POMs, and cases that intentionally trigger POM/parent validation failures.
Exercise plugin-related behaviors: including maven plugins in lockfiles, merging user-declared plugin dependencies into plugin resolution, and capturing plugin dependency graphs/checksums.
Test reduced lockfile logic and ordering sensitivity: scenarios where dependency ordering affects reduced result (e.g., log4j-related cases) and deterministic ordering assertions.

insights.title: Expected outputs are canonical and tightly coupled to implementation
insights.problem: Several tests compare generated outputs to committed expected files (lockfile.json, pom.lockfile.expected.xml). If generation behavior or sorting changes, tests will fail.
insights.root_cause: Integration tests assert exact equality against static expected resource files committed in this folder.
insights.solution: When modifying generation, freezing, ordering, or checksum resolution logic, update the corresponding expected fixture files in this directory to keep tests consistent; otherwise integration tests will detect regressions.

insights.title: Many fixtures rely on local checksumMode; remote-resolution tests explicitly require network repositories
insights.problem: Tests assume local checksums for determinism, but some fixtures (remoteRepositoryShouldResolve, resolvedFieldShouldResolve, externalParentPom) depend on fetching artifacts or metadata from remote repositories.
insights.root_cause: Mix of fixtures aimed at isolating checksum computation (local) and fixtures explicitly testing remote repository resolution.
insights.solution: Preserve checksumMode and repository configuration in fixtures; ensure CI/integration test environment allows network access for remote-resolving fixtures and account for platform-dependent artifacts when running these tests.

insights.title: Relative vs external parent POMs are explicitly tested and encoded
insights.problem: Fixtures model both relative parent resolution (multi-module layouts) and external parents; failures are intentionally provoked for validation tests.
insights.root_cause: Lockfile generation captures parent chain and checksums/resolved fields; different resolution strategies produce different lockfile content.
insights.solution: Any change to parent resolution, checksum capture for parents, or parent path handling must consider these fixtures; maintain relativePath semantics and resolved-parent recording or update expected outputs accordingly.

insights.title: Plugin inclusion and user-declared plugin dependency merging is validated
insights.problem: Tests exercise includeMavenPlugins and merging of user-declared plugin dependencies; output includes plugin metadata and plugin dependency graphs/checksums.
insights.root_cause: Lockfile format contains a mavenPlugins section capturing resolved plugin coordinates, repositoryOrigin, plugin checksum and plugin dependency nodes.
insights.solution: Preserve the shape and content assumptions of plugin entries in lockfile output; when changing plugin resolution or how user-declared dependencies are merged, update the fixtures that assert plugin-related outputs.

insights.title: Deterministic ordering is required by tests
insights.problem: Ordered-lockfile and freeze tests assert deterministic ordering of dependencies (including classifier handling). Non-deterministic iteration or unstable comparators will break tests.
insights.root_cause: Integration tests compare serialized outputs that depend on deterministic ordering of collections.
insights.solution: Maintain stable sorting/comparator behavior for dependency nodes, classifiers, repository lists, and any serialized lists used in lockfile/POM outputs; if algorithmic changes affect ordering, update expected fixtures.

insights.title: Reduced lockfile logic depends on dependency order and shading behavior
insights.problem: Two similar fixtures (reduceLog4jAffected / NotAffected) only differ by dependency ordering and expect different reduced outputs; shading interactions are simulated via maven-shade-plugin.
insights.root_cause: Reduction algorithm interprets transitive/ordering/shade interactions which can change which artifacts are included in the reduced lockfile.
insights.solution: When editing reduction behavior or interactions with shading, validate both order-sensitive fixtures and keep their expected outputs synchronized with algorithmic intent.

key_files.why: Canonical expected lockfile used to assert freeze behavior when dependencyManagement entries must be respected and included in frozen outputs.
Expected frozen POM for a project without dependencyManagement; tests compare generated frozen POM to this file to validate pinning/synthesizing behavior.
Exercise relative parent resolution in a multi-module layout; changes to parent handling must preserve semantics required by this fixture.
Fixture that records artifacts resolved from a remote repository and central; used to verify repository-origin and resolved URL fields are captured in lockfiles.
Models a plugin with an explicit user-declared dependency to validate merging behavior between plugin POM defaults and user overrides during plugin resolution.

construct_relationships: Each pom.xml fixture configures the maven-lockfile plugin with specific goals and options (checksumMode, includeMavenPlugins, reduced, exactVersionStrings) that drive how LockFileFacade and checksum calculators will be exercised by tests.

Expected files (lockfile.json, pom.lockfile.expected.xml) are the canonical outputs that integration tests compare to generated outputs from the plugin when run against the corresponding input POM fixtures.

Relative parent POM fixtures form parent-child pairs (relativeParentPom parent and child-module) where the child module's relativePath must resolve to the parent POM for generate goal tests to succeed.

Plugin-related fixtures (pluginProject, pluginUserDependency) configure includeMavenPlugins and user-declared plugin dependencies; tests expect plugin metadata and plugin dependency graphs to appear in generated lockfiles.

Reduce-related fixtures pair two similar POMs differing in dependency declaration order; the reduction logic (and any shading simulation via maven-shade-plugin) must yield different reduced lockfile outputs that the tests assert.
notable_relationships: These resources are consumed by the integration test harness in src/test (upstream code changes to LockFileFacade, checksum calculators, or serialization will directly affect test outcomes here).

Remote-repository fixtures require network access and rely on external repositories; CI environments that block egress or use mirrors will affect those tests and may cause mismatches in resolved URLs or repositoryIds.

Platform-dependent artifact checksums and classifier handling can cause divergent test results across OS/architecture matrix runs; the fixtures assume deterministic checksum calculation for the selected checksumMode.
Frozen POM expectations are downstream consumers of dependency resolution and version-pinning logic; any change in dependency resolution/version-selection impacts the expected frozen-POM files here.
recommendations: Do not rename, remove, or move fixture files without updating the integration tests that reference them; tests rely on exact paths and file contents.

When changing behavior that affects generated lockfile or frozen POM shape (fields, ordering, resolved-url/repositoryId, plugin entries, checksum algorithm), update corresponding expected files in this directory and re-run the integration test suite.

Respect checksumMode settings in the fixtures (many use local) and verify test runs under CI reproduce the intended network/no-network assumptions; run remote-repository-related tests only in environments that allow access to the declared repositories.
Preserve deterministic ordering and stable comparators used for serialization; if you alter ordering logic, validate the orderedLockfile fixture and other expected outputs.

Run the full integration test suite (the project's ITs) after edits that touch generation, resolution, checksum calculation, plugin resolution, parent handling, or freeze/reduce logic to catch mismatches with these fixtures.

maven_plugin/src/test.description: This subtree holds the unit and integration tests that verify the maven-lockfile plugin's behavior end-to-end and at the model/utility level. Tests exercise JSON serialization, checksum resolution (local repo layout and snapshot handling), data-model equality and ordering, value-object validation, MavenScope parsing defaults, plugin resolution and freeze behavior using real Maven runs and fixture projects. These tests encode the expected external-facing outputs and invariants that guard regressions across core flows.
key_behaviors: Serialization and deterministic JSON output for model objects (including handling of Guava collection views and avoiding spurious literal 'null').
Local repository checksum/resolved URL composition, with special handling for SNAPSHOT baseVersion vs timestamped artifacts and extraction of repository IDs from _remote.repositories files.
Data-model invariants: equality and ordering semantics for LockFile and MavenPlugin, and strict value-object validation for GroupId, ArtifactId, VersionNumber and MavenScope parsing/defaults.

End-to-end integration flows: invoking plugin goals (generate, validate, freeze) against fixture Maven projects, reading generated lockfile.json and pom.lockfile.xml, and comparing against expected fixtures.
Plugin resolution flows in integration: inclusion of Maven plugins, merging user-declared plugin dependencies, and BOM/parent-POM resolution (including external parents) are exercised by IT fixtures.

insights.title: Guava set-view serialization can produce undesired output
insights.problem: Serializing a Guava Sets.difference view directly led to JSON containing the literal string "null" in earlier runs.
insights.root_cause: JSON serializer configuration or handling did not correctly handle Guava collection view implementations, and a view type was serialized without materializing into a standard concrete collection.
insights.solution: Tests materialize Guava views into concrete HashSet instances before serialization; maintain JsonUtils behavior that safely serializes Sets and guard future changes that introduce alternative collection views.

insights.title: SNAPSHOT resolved URL composition must use baseVersion directory
insights.problem: Timestamped SNAPSHOT artifact filenames must still reference the baseVersion directory portion (e.g., 1.0-SNAPSHOT) for resolved URL construction; incorrect handling will misrecord resolved fields.
insights.root_cause: Local repository layout separates directory (baseVersion) and filename (timestamped copy); naive use of artifact version can produce wrong directories.
insights.solution: FileSystem checksum logic and tests assert baseVersion usage for SNAPSHOT directories and correct repository id extraction from _remote.repositories files; maintain these semantics in checksum calculators.

insights.title: LockFile equality must be order-insensitive
insights.problem: Tests and consumers expect logically equivalent LockFile instances to compare equal regardless of insertion order of dependencies, plugins, or BOMs.
insights.root_cause: Collections in the data model were (or are) represented as Sets to enforce order-insensitivity; earlier code or tests assumed Lists and index-based ordering.
insights.solution: Treat dependency/plugin/BOM collections as Sets in equality contracts and ensure tests validate equality independently of insertion order; any model changes that affect equals/hashCode must preserve this behavior.

insights.title: New features require end-to-end integration assertions
insights.problem: BOMs, parent POMs, plugin dependencies, resolved fields, and remote checksum modes introduce behaviors that unit tests alone don't catch.
insights.root_cause: These features interact with Maven's resolver and repository layout; unit tests cannot fully simulate real Maven resolution semantics and file outputs.
insights.solution: Integration tests run real Maven builds against fixture projects and assert produced lockfile/pom outputs; maintain and update IT fixtures and expected outputs when resolution behavior changes.

insights.title: Value objects must reject invalid inputs and preserve exact strings
insights.problem: GroupId, ArtifactId, and VersionNumber are relied upon to be non-null/non-empty and for VersionNumber to preserve exact strings (including snapshots/timestamps).
insights.root_cause: Lockfile output and comparisons depend on canonical, stable identity/value representations; malformed or normalized values could break reproducibility.
insights.solution: Unit tests validate constructors/rejection rules and exact value preservation; any edits to these types must keep the same validation and retention semantics.

insights.title: Scope parsing must default to COMPILE for null/empty
insights.problem: Other components rely on MavenScope.fromString(null or empty) returning COMPILE; deviating behavior would change lockfile scope entries.
insights.root_cause: Null/empty inputs occur in POMs or resolver outputs; a default improves stability and compatibility.
insights.solution: Tests assert round-trip parsing for all enum values and default behavior; preserve these defaults when modifying scope-related code.

key_files.why: Primary end-to-end integration suite that runs real Maven builds against fixture projects and asserts generated lockfile/pom outputs—guards cross-cutting resolution and freeze behaviors.
Validates LockFile model equality and order-insensitivity for dependencies/plugins/BOMs—protects a core invariant relied upon by CI checks and outputs.
Verifies local-repo checksum/resolved URL composition, especially SNAPSHOT baseVersion behavior and repository id extraction from _remote.repositories.
Ensures JsonUtils serializes Sets (including materialized Guava views) correctly and does not emit literal 'null'—important for deterministic lockfile JSON output.
Asserts deterministic compareTo ordering for MavenPlugin, which affects ordering in outputs and equality comparisons used by tests and consumers.

construct_relationships: JsonUtils is used to serialize Set<DependencyNode> and other model types; tests ensure concrete Sets (not Guava view types) are serialized without producing "null" literals.

FileSystemChecksumCalculator composes resolved URL strings from artifact metadata and local repository layout and is validated by FileSystemChecksumCalculatorTest which simulates _remote.repositories mappings.

LockFile model (including Pom, MavenPlugin, ResolvedUrl, RepositoryId) is constructed by plugin code under test and is asserted by LockfileTest and IntegrationTestsIT for equality, ordering, and content correctness.

IntegrationTestsIT invokes real Maven executions (generate/validate/freeze goals) and validates outputs that depend on checksum calculators, BOM resolution, plugin resolution, and freeze logic implemented in main code.
MavenPlugin.compareTo (tested by MavenPluginTest) dictates ordering semantics that affect deterministic output ordering asserted by orderedLockfile integration fixtures.
notable_relationships: Integration tests depend on fixtures in src/test/resources-its and on the main plugin implementation under src/main; changes to model shapes, serialization, checksum behavior, or resolution flows in main code will require updating both unit and IT expectations.

Integration tests exercise network/repository resolution semantics (external parents, BOMs, remote checksum modes); CI environment constraints (egress allowlists, platform differences) can affect test outcomes—fixtures and expected outputs assume certain resolution behaviors.
Deterministic ordering and Set-based equality in tests imply that any change to equals/hashCode or to how collections are serialized will ripple into multiple tests and resource expectations.

Integration test harness uses the com.soebes Maven IT extension and inspects files on disk produced by real Maven runs; these tests are sensitive to build lifecycle, plugin configuration, and produced artifact file paths.
recommendations: When modifying production code in this module, run both unit tests and the integration test suite (mvn test and the configured IT runner) because fixtures validate real Maven runs and serialized outputs.
Preserve existing serialization semantics: JsonUtils must continue to handle Sets and materialized Guava views; verify any changes by running JsonUtilsTest and IT scenarios that read lockfile.json.

If changing checksum calculation, SNAPSHOT handling, or resolved-field composition, update FileSystemChecksumCalculatorTest and re-run ITs that assert resolved URLs and repository ids (fixtures expect specific formats).

Any change to data-model classes (equals/hashCode, compareTo, collection types, or fields like Pom/ResolvedUrl/RepositoryId) requires updating LockfileTest, MavenPluginTest, and expected IT resources (lockfile.json, pom.lockfile.expected.xml).

Be aware of environment- and platform-sensitive behaviors (native classifiers, remote checksum mode, network access) when running integration tests in CI; ensure test runs replicate CI constraints or adjust expectations in fixtures accordingly.

When editing tests or fixtures in src/test/resources-its, maintain the mapping between the fixture POMs and their corresponding expected outputs; do not assume list ordering in tests where models now use Sets—verify equality/order-insensitivity is preserved.

.github/workflows.description: This folder contains GitHub Actions workflows that orchestrate CI for the project: lockfile generation/validation, code quality/reproducibility checks, docs regeneration, release automation (JReleaser), manual/regeneration triggers, and smoke tests. These workflows coordinate external actions, runner environments (Java/Maven), tokens/permissions, and working directories; they directly affect what CI runs, what files may be committed back to the repo, and which runtime constraints must be satisfied for successful builds and releases.
key_behaviors: Scheduled and event-driven CI runs that generate and validate the Maven lockfile using a pinned maven-lockfile action and may commit updates back to the repository.
Code-quality/reproducibility verification jobs that install Java/Maven, run POM-quality checks and repeatable-build checks for the Maven plugin.
Documentation regeneration workflow that updates templated docs (README/action.yml) automatically on PRs and/or on-demand.
Release workflow (JReleaser) that builds, may run the maven-lockfile action as part of release, and needs write permissions and a compatible Java runtime.
Smoke-test workflow that executes scripts (via jbang) and Maven commands in a specific working-directory with environment variables passed through.

insights.title: Workflows reference older pinned action SHAs
insights.problem: Several workflows pin older SHAs or versions of the maven-lockfile action, missing fixes or new features.
insights.root_cause: Workflows were not updated when the action was released with fixes/features; pinning to stable tags/SHAs was left outdated.
insights.solution: Treat action version pins as a first-class maintenance concern: ensure workflows reference compatible, intentional versions and recognize that different workflows must be kept in sync.

insights.title: No default PR run for lockfile validation
insights.problem: The repository lacked a workflow that runs the maven-lockfile action on pull requests by default, reducing early detection of lockfile regressions.
insights.root_cause: Lockfile validation/generation was exercised primarily on push or release events, not on PR events.
insights.solution: When changing workflows or adding new checks, verify that the intended events (push, pull_request, workflow_dispatch) are configured so validation runs at the right time.

insights.title: CI jobs rely on explicit Java/Maven setup and aligned Java versions
insights.problem: Some workflows omitted explicit Maven setup or used mismatched Java versions (Java 11 vs 17), causing tool compatibility and reproducibility issues.
insights.root_cause: Assumptions about runner defaults or copy-pasted workflow steps created inconsistencies across jobs.
insights.solution: Ensure each job explicitly sets required Java and Maven versions consistent with tools used (JReleaser, plugin build, action requirements) and treat runtime alignment as critical.

insights.title: Release workflows need write permission and correct token for committing lockfile changes
insights.problem: Release steps that should commit updated lockfiles lacked write permission or used the default token; commits might not be created or may not retrigger CI as intended.
insights.root_cause: Default GITHUB_TOKEN restrictions and workflow permissions were not accounted for when workflows were authored.
insights.solution: Workflows that must commit back must request repository write permissions and use an appropriate token (e.g., a personal access token) and callers must be aware of the token behavior (PRs, forks, reruns).

insights.title: Smoke tests and scripts depend on working-directory and environment variables
insights.problem: Smoke-tests assumed the mvn wrapper and scripts are at specific locations and did not set working-directory or correctly pass env vars to jbang, causing step failures.
insights.root_cause: Steps relied on repository layout or implicit environment propagation rather than explicit working-directory and env declarations.
insights.solution: Jobs must set working-directory and explicitly pass/shape environment variables expected by scripts; path assumptions and script invocations must be validated in CI.

insights.title: Documentation templates need automated regeneration on PRs
insights.problem: Templated docs and action metadata (README/action.yml) were not automatically regenerated during PRs, risking drift between code and published documentation.
insights.root_cause: No dedicated workflow existed to run the templating/generation step on PR events.
insights.solution: Include an explicit docs-generation workflow trigger for PRs and document that changes to templates may require running that workflow to keep docs in sync.

key_files.why: Primary workflow for generating/validating the maven lockfile; controls which action version is used and whether lockfile updates are committed.
Release orchestration that must be granted write permissions and the correct Java runtime; ties releases to lockfile actions and docs generation.
Holds reproducibility and POM-quality checks; needs explicit Maven and Java setup to ensure plugin builds are reproducible.
Executes runtime smoke tests and scripts (jbang); depends on working-directory and environment variables for successful runs.
Provides a manual or scheduled trigger to regenerate lockfiles; impacts when and how lockfiles are updated outside push/release events.

construct_relationships: Lockfile.yml invokes the maven-lockfile GitHub Action; the action version pinned here must be consistent with versions assumed by release workflows.
jreleaser.yml runs release steps that may call the same maven-lockfile logic and also triggers docs generation; it must align Java runtime and permissions with those other workflows.
code-qualitiy.yml and Lockfile.yml both require Maven to be set up in the runner and therefore share the need to configure Java/Maven versions and runner environment correctly.
smoke-tests.yml runs scripts (jbang) that depend on repository layout and the presence of the mvnw wrapper; working-directory settings in smoke-tests.yml must match where those files live.
regenerate-lockfile.yml and Lockfile.yml are operationally related: one is a manual/dispatch trigger and the other is the main automation — both affect lockfile.json and commit behavior.
notable_relationships: These workflows can modify repository state (lockfile.json, docs) and therefore interact with repo permissions, token usage patterns (GITHUB_TOKEN vs personal tokens), and CI re-run behavior.
Workflows depend on specific Java and Maven versions that must be compatible with project build logic, the Maven plugin code, and third-party tools like JReleaser and jbang.

Action version pins create an implicit dependency on external action releases; updating action versions in one workflow may necessitate parallel updates in other workflows that assume the same behavior.
Docs generation and action metadata (README/action.yml) are produced from repository sources; automated regeneration on PRs is tied to workflows in this folder and affects release artifacts.
recommendations: When editing any workflow, verify and document the intended runner Java and Maven versions for that job; treat runtime alignment as mandatory for reproducible CI.
Check and explicitly set workflow permissions and token use for steps that must commit files: ensure write access is declared and be aware of GITHUB_TOKEN behavior vs personal tokens for PRs/forks.
Keep action version pins consistent across workflows that interoperate (maven-lockfile, jbang-related actions); confirm compatibility before changing pins.
Ensure job steps explicitly set working-directory and required environment variables for scripts (jbang, mvnw) so path assumptions are not implicit.

When modifying workflows that generate or commit files (lockfile.json, README/action.yml), consider the downstream effects: which jobs depend on those artifacts and whether CI re-runs or protected-branch rules will be triggered.

Run the affected CI jobs locally or in a throwaway branch after edits: at minimum execute lockfile generation, code-quality/reproducibility checks, smoke tests, and the release job path to validate changes.

maven_plugin.description: maven_plugin contains the Maven plugin implementation and surrounding build model that produce, validate, and freeze JSON lockfiles for Maven projects. It orchestrates dependency-graph collection, checksum resolution (local or remote), plugin and BOM resolution, and JSON (de)serialization of the lockfile model; correctness here is critical for reproducible builds, CI validation, and stable lockfile schemas consumed by downstream workflows.
key_behaviors: Generate: collect project dependency graph + parent POM chain, compute checksums (local or remote), optionally collect plugin dependency trees and BOMs, assemble LockFile model and serialize to JSON.

Validate: read on-disk lockfile, reconstruct resolution using stored Config, recompute checksums (force local when configured), compare generated model to persisted lockfile and enforce configured validation policies.

Freeze: read lockfile, compute selected direct and transitive versions, write a frozen POM with pinned direct dependency versions and dependencyManagement entries; optionally include plugin dependencies.

Checksum resolution: provide pluggable strategies (FileSystem or Remote) with prewarm/caching hooks, consistent checksum algorithm selection, and resolved repository metadata returned as RepositoryInformation.
Serialization: use a centralized Gson configuration to (de)serialize many small typed value-objects as compact JSON primitives, ensuring stable JSON schema and ordering for deterministic diffs.

insights.title: Centralized Gson adapters ensure compact/stable lockfile JSON
insights.problem: Domain value objects (ArtifactType, Classifier, MavenScope, etc.) were serialized inconsistently or as verbose objects, breaking compactness and compatibility.
insights.root_cause: Multiple ad-hoc (de)serialization points and missing adapters for new typed wrappers.
insights.solution: Register explicit Gson adapters in a single JsonUtils provider and update adapters whenever new value objects are added; maintain stable serialized names and lenient parsing where backward compatibility is required.

insights.title: Checksum mode behavior must be deterministic and efficient
insights.problem: Validation and generation sometimes used different checksum modes, produced network-bound or inconsistent results, and repeated network calls caused slow runs.
insights.root_cause: Checksum selection logic scattered and calculators lacked prewarm/caching; snapshot path resolution used timestamped versions or wrong extension/classifier handling.
insights.solution: Use a single checksum factory (from Mojo) and ensure calculators implement prewarm, caching, baseVersion-based path resolution and classifier/extension handling so generation and validation reproduce the same results efficiently.

insights.title: SNAPSHOT artifact path resolution must use baseVersion
insights.problem: Local and remote resolution looked up snapshot artifacts using timestamped version strings and produced incorrect paths or failed HEAD/GET requests.
insights.root_cause: Code used artifact.getVersion() rather than getBaseVersion(), and file name construction ignored artifact handler extension and classifiers.
insights.solution: Resolve snapshot directory and filenames using baseVersion and artifact handler extension; include classifier in file name resolution logic for both local and remote calculators.

insights.title: Non-Mojo components depend on Maven logging context
insights.problem: Utility and calculator classes logged via different mechanisms or lacked access to Maven's Mojo log, producing inconsistent or missing log messages.
insights.root_cause: PluginLogManager was not initialized by Mojos; utilities used standalone loggers.
insights.solution: Mojos must set PluginLogManager.setLog(getLog()) before delegating work so non-Mojo classes log through Maven's logging channel.

insights.title: Deterministic ordering and equality are required for stable lockfiles and caching
insights.problem: Lockfile JSON diffs and in-memory caches were unstable because some domain objects lacked compareTo/equals/hashCode or sets preserved insertion order inconsistently.
insights.root_cause: Value objects and collection choices did not enforce deterministic ordering or equality semantics.
insights.solution: Make domain types comparable and define equals/hashCode; use sorted/ordered collections where stable diffs are required and normalize null collections to empty sets before serialization.

insights.title: Plugin resolution must capture full metadata for later validation
insights.problem: Lockfiles lacked plugin resolved URLs, repository ids, checksums, and plugin-dependency trees, preventing full validation of build-time artifacts.
insights.root_cause: Initial model and facade logic omitted plugin resolved location and plugin dependency resolution to keep generation simpler.
insights.solution: Record MavenPlugin resolved url, repository id, checksum+algorithm and dependency nodes in the LockFile model; ensure LockFileFacade and checksum calculators can produce that data.

key_files.why: Central orchestration for building LockFile: collects dependency graph, parent POMs, BOMs and plugin metadata; primary integration point between Maven resolution and checksum calculators.
Provides common Mojo context, config and checksum factory used by concrete goals; changes here affect all goals' configuration, checksum selection, and project-building request construction.
Entrypoint for generating lockfiles; orchestrates reading existing lockfile, merging Config, delegating to LockFileFacade and writing JSON output.
Entrypoint for validation: reads lockfile, reconstructs resolution with stored Config, and enforces validation policies; affects CI failures/allowances.
Defines the checksum API and prewarm hook used by Remote/FileSystem implementations; changes here alter how checksums are computed, cached, and exposed to the LockFile model.

construct_relationships: GenerateLockFileMojo and ValidateChecksumMojo obtain Config and checksum calculator from AbstractLockfileMojo and delegate the heavy lifting to LockFileFacade.

LockFileFacade uses Maven's DependencyCollectorBuilder/DependencyResolver and ProjectBuildingRequest (created by AbstractLockfileMojo) to build a dependency graph and to ask a supplied AbstractChecksumCalculator for artifact and POM checksums and RepositoryInformation.

AbstractChecksumCalculator is an abstraction implemented by FileSystemChecksumCalculator and RemoteChecksumCalculator; LockFileFacade treats calculators interchangeably but relies on correct prewarm/caching and resolved-url semantics.

JsonUtils provides Gson configuration for serializing/deserializing data model classes (LockFile, MavenPlugin, Pom, ArtifactType, Classifier, Environment, Config); LockFileFacade/ Mojos rely on that serialization to read/write lockfile.json.

FreezeDependencyMojo reads the LockFile model (LockFile.readLockFile) and maps dependency graph nodes into Maven Dependency entries and dependencyManagement; it also interacts with PluginLogManager to ensure consistent logging across utility code.
notable_relationships: maven_plugin/pom.xml drives build and published coordinates; changing plugin artifact coordinates or plugin versions can affect consumers and CI releases.
RemoteChecksumCalculator performs network calls to external Maven repositories; its behavior impacts CI runtime, potential flakiness, and platform-dependent checksum differences.

Lockfile JSON is consumed by CI workflows (e.g., .github/workflows/Lockfile.yml) and other repository tools; schema changes (serialized names, added/removed fields) must preserve backward compatibility or be coordinated with downstream consumers.

The plugin depends on Maven internals (DependencyCollectorBuilder, DependencyResolver, ProjectBuildingRequest) and local repository layout conventions (including _remote.repositories parsing); changes must respect Maven resolution semantics and local repo file layouts.
Gson SerializedName usage (ChecksumModes, etc.) is part of stable serialized contract—alterations here affect persisted Config and lockfile compatibility.
recommendations: Preserve JSON serialization compatibility: update JsonUtils whenever adding/removing typed wrappers and keep SerializedName strings stable; run serialization-based tests after changes.
Ensure Mojos always initialize PluginLogManager.setLog(getLog()) before invoking non-Mojo code so logs appear in Maven output.

When modifying checksum behavior, respect the prewarm hook and caching contract; ensure Remote and FileSystem calculators continue to return consistent RepositoryInformation and use baseVersion, artifact handler extension, and classifier when resolving paths.

Maintain deterministic ordering and equality for any domain value objects used in sets/maps or serialized output (implement Comparable and equals/hashCode); changing collection types or ordering will affect lockfile diffs.

When changing Config or LockFile schema, consider backward compatibility: LockFile.readLockFile and getConfigPath must still parse older lockfiles or migration logic must be provided; update consumers (Validate/Freeze) accordingly.

Run unit and integration tests plus lifecycle verification: mvn test and mvn verify (failsafe) for the module; apply project formatting (mvn spotless:apply) to avoid CI failures; test on multiple platforms if checksum or snapshot handling changes due to platform-dependent artifacts.

Be aware of CI behavior around committing lockfiles: changing behavior that writes/updates lockfile.json may require coordination with GitHub Action token usage and platform matrix differences for checksum validation.

..description: Repository root that provides a reusable GitHub Action and a Maven plugin implementation to generate, validate, and freeze a JSON dependency lockfile for Maven projects. The Action (action.yml) invokes the maven-lockfile plugin module (maven_plugin) and the plugin module contains Mojos, orchestration (LockFileFacade), checksum resolution strategies, the lockfile data model, and JSON (de)serialization logic required for producing deterministic lockfiles consumed by CI and downstream tooling.
key_behaviors: CI orchestration: the composite GitHub Action checks out code, detects changes (pom.xml and optionally workflow file), maps inputs to -D Maven properties, invokes the plugin goals (generate/validate), and can commit updated lockfiles back to the repo.

Lockfile generation: GenerateLockFileMojo (via AbstractLockfileMojo) delegates to LockFileFacade to collect a dependency graph, construct the parent POM chain, optionally resolve BOMs and plugin dependency subgraphs, compute checksums (local or remote), assemble a LockFile model, and serialize it using JsonUtils.

Validation: ValidateChecksumMojo replays generation using serialized Config from the lockfile (or mojo config), forces appropriate checksum mode for deterministic comparison, compares produced LockFile to on-disk lockfile, and enforces configured validation policies.

Freeze: FreezeDependencyMojo reads a lockfile, computes selected direct/transitive versions (nearest version resolution), optionally includes plugin dependencies, and writes a frozen POM with direct versions pinned and transitive dependencies added to dependencyManagement.

Checksum resolution: AbstractChecksumCalculator is the abstraction used by FileSystemChecksumCalculator and RemoteChecksumCalculator; remote mode supports caching and parallel pre-warm while filesystem mode reads local repository state and _remote.repositories to infer resolved URLs and repository ids.

insights.title: Local vs Remote modes must be deterministic and performant
insights.problem: Validation or generation using the wrong checksum mode or inefficient remote fetches produced inconsistent or slow lockfile runs.
insights.root_cause: Mixed usage of remote vs local checksum sources, lack of prewarm/caching, and per-request HttpClient creation for remote resolution.
insights.solution: Respect the configured ChecksumModes and checksum algorithm stored in Config; remote calculators should prewarm and cache network results; validation should default to forced local checksums to avoid network variance and perf issues.

insights.title: Incorrect path resolution for SNAPSHOT artifacts
insights.problem: Snapshot artifacts were resolved using timestamped versions or wrong filename heuristics, causing missing checksum/URL lookups and incorrect resolved fields.
insights.root_cause: Code used artifact.getVersion() (possibly timestamped) instead of artifact.getBaseVersion() and ignored artifact handler extension/classifier when building paths.
insights.solution: When mapping artifacts to repository paths or requesting remote checksum/HEAD resources use baseVersion and artifact handler extension and include classifier when present so both local and remote resolution match repository layouts.

insights.title: Lockfile JSON compactness and schema stability
insights.problem: New value types (ArtifactType, Classifier, MavenScope, etc.) and alterations to the LockFile shape (Config, boms) require consistent Gson adapters and serialized names to keep lockfile compatibility and compactness.
insights.root_cause: Domain primitives serialized as objects or with inconsistent names led to larger or incompatible JSON and brittle parsing across plugin versions.
insights.solution: Centralize Gson adapters in JsonUtils, serialize domain wrappers as primitives (or omit default jar packaging), annotate ChecksumModes with serialized names, and preserve lenient parsing where backwards compatibility is required.

insights.title: Non-Mojo classes expect a Maven Log
insights.problem: Non-Mojo components used PluginLogManager.getLog() but Mojos did not always initialize PluginLogManager, causing missing or inconsistent logs and differing behavior between CLI and tests.
insights.root_cause: PluginLogManager was not set by Mojos before invoking shared components (checksum calculators, LockFileFacade).
insights.solution: Mojos must set PluginLogManager.setLog(getLog()) before delegating to shared classes so logging behavior is consistent across generate/validate/freeze flows.

insights.title: Value objects need stable equality for caching and comparisons
insights.problem: Repository/Resolved value objects lacked equals/hashCode making cache keys and set/map membership unreliable.
insights.root_cause: Plain container objects returned multiple primitives separately and lacked proper equality/hash semantics for use in caches or collections.
insights.solution: Encapsulate related resolved fields into immutable value objects (RepositoryInformation) and implement equals/hashCode to allow reliable caching/comparisons across checksum resolvers and facade logic.

insights.title: Action inputs and commit behavior are opinionated
insights.problem: The composite action hard-coded commit author, commit message defaults and only watched pom.xml for changes by default, which limited user control and could misattribute commits or miss changes that require regeneration.
insights.root_cause: Action hard-coded EndBug/add-and-commit defaults and monitored only pom.xml unless workflow-filename input was used.
insights.solution: Action maps inputs to Maven properties; when relying on the Action, be aware that commit behavior (token, author, message) and watched files are configurable inputs and edits to action.yml should preserve or document these input mappings and effects.

insights.title: Freeze must account for plugin dependencies and exact version formats
insights.problem: Earlier freeze output omitted plugin dependencies and always emitted plain version strings, producing incomplete or insufficiently pinned POMs for some consumers.
insights.root_cause: Freeze goal previously only processed project dependencies and lacked options for exact version formatting and plugin merging.
insights.solution: Freeze logic now supports including plugin dependencies and an exactVersionStrings option; edits must respect those flags and ensure dependencyManagement entries and plugin merges are written in a deterministic order.

key_files.why: Composite GitHub Action that maps inputs to Maven properties, detects changed files, runs generate/validate goals, and optionally commits updated lockfiles — edits here affect CI invocation and commit semantics.
Central orchestration that collects dependency graph, parent POM chain, plugin metadata and BOMs, and composes the LockFile model used by generate/validate/freeze Mojos.
Common Mojo base that constructs Config, selects checksum calculators, builds ProjectBuildingRequests, and initializes environment metadata — changes affect all concrete goals.
Network-bound checksum/resolution implementation offering caching and prewarm semantics; impacts generation performance and determinism when remote checksums are used.
Single place registering Gson adapters for domain types — must be kept consistent to preserve lockfile JSON schema, compactness, and backwards compatibility.

construct_relationships: GenerateLockFileMojo, ValidateChecksumMojo and FreezeDependencyMojo extend AbstractLockfileMojo to obtain Maven context and construct Config and checksum calculators.

Mojos delegate orchestration to LockFileFacade which uses DependencyCollectorBuilder/DependencyResolver to build a DependencyGraph and invokes an AbstractChecksumCalculator implementation to attach checksums and resolved repository information to nodes.

AbstractChecksumCalculator is implemented by FileSystemChecksumCalculator and RemoteChecksumCalculator; LockFileFacade chooses and may call prewarmArtifactCache on the chosen calculator prior to heavy resolution.

JsonUtils serializes/deserializes LockFile and related data value objects (ArtifactType, Classifier, GroupId, ArtifactId, Config, Environment) and must match the shapes produced by the LockFile model and consumed by Mojos.
RepositoryInformation is produced by checksum calculators and used by LockFileFacade and LockFile model to represent resolved URL + repository id as a single unit for caching and equality checks.
notable_relationships: action.yml -> maven_plugin: the composite GitHub Action invokes plugin goals with -D properties derived from inputs; changes to property names or expected plugin parameters must be kept aligned between action.yml and AbstractLockfileMojo/GenerateLockFileMojo.

maven_plugin/pom.xml -> build & test behavior: POM changes (version pins, plugin versions, packaging) affect plugin compilation, generated mojo descriptors, and integration-test execution via failsafe; CI workflows depend on these artifacts.

Lockfile JSON -> downstream consumers: lockfile.json schema (field names, presence/absence of packaging/type, classifiers, Config and boms) is consumed by validate and freeze goals and by external CI workflows; schema changes impact validation reproducibility and freeze output.

Platform/CI matrix -> checksum determinism: platform-specific artifacts and SNAPSHOT layouts can cause different checksums across OS/arch/runner matrix; CI action and validation logic are sensitive to these platform-dependent differences.

Git commit workflow -> CI re-triggering: action's commit step may require a personal token (not default GITHUB_TOKEN) to commit updates in PR workflows and retrigger CI as intended; this affects how the action is configured in repositories.
recommendations: When editing Mojos or LockFileFacade preserve Config serialization and the Contract used to reconstruct generation settings from existing lockfiles; ensure backward-compatible Gson names/enum serialized names are retained.

Any changes touching checksum resolution must maintain baseVersion handling, classifier and artifact extension handling, and must keep RepositoryInformation equality/hashing intact for caches; validate both local and remote modes and re-run integration tests that simulate SNAPSHOT and classifier cases.
If touching logging or PluginLogManager usage, ensure every Mojo initializes PluginLogManager.setLog(getLog()) before invoking shared non-Mojo classes so logging side-effects remain consistent.

When modifying action.yml keep the mapping between Action inputs and -D Maven properties stable (or update Mojos to accept new property names) and preserve commit-related inputs (token, author, message) semantics if users rely on them.

Preserve JsonUtils adapters for all domain wrappers (ArtifactType, Classifier, MavenScope, VersionNumber, etc.) to avoid lockfile schema changes; run unit + integration serialization tests and verify lockfile round-trips.

Run full test matrix and formatting: execute mvn test, mvn failsafe:integration-test (where used), and mvn spotless:apply to ensure changes do not break builds, plugin descriptor generation, or CI expectations.
